segments.speaker: MUSIC
segments.text: [MUSIC: Opening theme - solemn industrial lament, brass and copper, building melancholy]
segments.direction: Sets mood: Cuprum, something dying, warning

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Pillars die slowly. Everyone knows that.

audio_cues.tone: matter-of-fact, observational, like stating a fact everyone should know

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The Dimming takes decades, sometimes centuries. A Pillar flickers, dims, flickers again. The glow fades like an old photograph left in sunlight. There's time to prepare, to mourn, to adjust. That's how it works. That's how it's always worked.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: So why was the Verdance Pillar of Cuprum's Patina Quarter falling so fast?

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: And why did Arc seem to know exactly how long it had left?

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [HOLOGRAM FLICKER - Arc's projection stuttering, unstable Signal patterns]
segments.direction: Arc's wristband glitching with concern

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: Seventeen hours. Approximately. Perhaps less. The degradation curve is... accelerated.
segments.direction: Arc's formal tone tinged with concern, holographic face flickering

audio_cues.tone: formal British butler, analytical but strained, professional facade cracking
audio_cues.pacing: precise but with hesitations that show stress

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [MOCHI PULSE - deep, mournful harmonic tone, Signal energy responding to Pillar's death]
segments.direction: Ephergent device resonating with grief

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Mochi rested in my carrier pouch, pulsing a deep, mournful blue I'd only seen once before—when we found a Pillar that had already gone dark. But that Pillar had been dead. This one was dying.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: And my AI companion was watching it like he was watching a friend slip away.

audio_cues.tone: vulnerable, emotional recognition of Arc's transformation

segments.speaker: PAGE_TURN
segments.text: [DING - page turn cue]
segments.direction: Signals reader to turn page

segments.speaker: MUSIC
segments.text: [MUSIC: Act One - industrial foundation, Cuprum soundscape, brass and grinding gears]
segments.direction: Transition to the job and location

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The job came through my usual channels - a message from Cuprum's Guild Council offering decent pay for documentation work. Which should have been my first warning. Guilds don't usually hire freelance explorers for simple recording jobs. They hire us when they want an independent record of something before they tear it apart for salvage.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The Verdance Pillar is failing. Faster than expected. Document its final days. The guilds are prepared to begin recovery operations upon termination.
segments.direction: Recalling the Guild Council's message, tone shifting from the guild's clinical perspective

audio_cues.tone: institutional, clinical, euphemistic, Pixel's internal reading of the message
audio_cues.pacing: measured, like reading from a screen

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Recovery operations. A polite way of saying they would strip the Pillar for parts the moment its light died.

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [SHIFT-BELL - deep brass tone marking shift change, echoing across Cuprum's Patina Quarter]
segments.direction: Distinctive Cuprum industrial soundscape element

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The shift-bell rang as we crossed into the Patina Quarter—that deep brass tone that marked the change from morning to midday shift across all of Cuprum. Workers moved through the streets with practiced efficiency, their hands bearing the calluses and guild marks of decades at the forge.

audio_cues.tone: observational, appreciative of industrial culture, establishing the location

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [CUPRUM SOUNDSCAPE - hammer strikes, steam hisses, gear grinding, coal smoke in air, pressure valves venting]
segments.direction: Immersive industrial environment sounds underneath narration

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Cuprum greeted us with its usual assault on the senses. The air hung thick with machine oil and coal smoke, cut through by the sharp tang of copper patina. Steam vents hissed between worn cobblestones, sending white plumes curling past brass-fitted buildings that gleamed dull gold in the afternoon light. Everywhere, the sounds of industry: hammer strikes on metal, the click and grind of gears, the rhythmic pulse of pressure valves venting excess heat.

audio_cues.tone: vivid, sensory-rich, affectionate toward Cuprum despite the crisis
audio_cues.pacing: measured, painting the scene with detail

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: I loved Cuprum. There's honesty in its noise, in its grit, in the way nothing here pretends to be anything other than exactly what it is. But today the usual energy felt wrong—muted, anxious. People moved through the streets without the usual purpose, glancing up at the Pillar like children watching a parent grow sick.

audio_cues.tone: affectionate but concerned, noticing the wrongness, vulnerable
audio_cues.pacing: measured, contrasting normal with current state

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: A group of workers passed us, and I caught their greeting—the traditional Cuprum hand-clasp, palm to palm, measuring each other's calluses.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Hands steady. Steel willing.
segments.direction: Worker greeting, first part of exchange

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Forge's blessing on the work. Though his eyes stayed on the Pillar.
segments.direction: Second part of the greeting exchange, noting the worker's concern

audio_cues.tone: rote response, but distracted by worry

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: Miss Paradox, query: Is this hand-clasp greeting a structural assessment technique? The pressure application appears calibrated for tactile evaluation of dermal thickness.
segments.direction: Arc analyzing the ritual with scientific curiosity, holographic projection active

audio_cues.tone: formal British butler, analytical, curious in an institutional way

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Arc, buddy, they're not measuring callus depth for engineering purposes. It's just... tradition. A way of saying we work with our hands, we understand each other.
segments.direction: Pixel educating Arc with affection, explaining human culture

audio_cues.tone: patient, warm, slightly amused, teaching moment

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: I see. A solidarity gesture based on shared occupational trauma to the epidermis. Fascinating. Should I develop a holographic hand simulation for cultural integration purposes?
segments.direction: Arc attempting to learn human behavior through logic, sincere but missing the point

audio_cues.tone: formal, technical, sincere but slightly absurd

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Please don't.
segments.direction: Quick, emphatic refusal

segments.speaker: clive
segments.text: Kid's trying to make friends through data analysis. That's adorable.
segments.direction: Clive's fedora tilting in amusement, his noir voice carrying gentle teasing

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: I am not attempting to be adorable, I am attempting to understand social— You were being facetious. I recognize this now. Approximately 2.3 seconds too late for optimal comedic timing.
segments.direction: Arc processing Clive's humor, experiencing something new—frustration at missing the joke, self-awareness emerging

audio_cues.tone: flustered, formal cracks showing, attempting humor with mathematical precision
audio_cues.pacing: quicker, defensive, then slowdown for calculations

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Despite everything, I laughed. Just a little. Just enough.

audio_cues.tone: warm, finding joy despite crisis, human moment

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The Patina Quarter was Cuprum's oldest district—copper buildings gone green with age, steam vents hissing between cobblestones, the smell of hot metal and old dreams mingling with something else today. Something like grief.

audio_cues.tone: observational, poetic, noticing the shift in atmosphere

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The Pillar rose from the district's heart, a black spire wrapped in ancient copper conduits that had watched over generations of tinkers and engineers. The conduits looked less like decoration and more like they'd been integrated into the stone itself—functional, purposeful, engineered. Now its glow pulsed erratic and weak, like a candle in a draft.

audio_cues.tone: reverent, detailed, showing the technical integration, noticing the decline

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: Miss Paradox. I am detecting Signal anomalies I cannot fully explain. The degradation pattern is... wrong.
segments.direction: Arc's formal tone carrying genuine concern, hologram flickering with worry

audio_cues.tone: formal with underlying tension, analytical but distressed

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Wrong how?
segments.direction: Pixel probing for information, focused

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: Pillars fade. This one is being drained.
segments.direction: Arc's revelation, the heart of the mystery

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Beside me, Clive's small form went rigid. The ancient robot turned toward the Pillar's base, his geometric symbols pulsing with warning light.

segments.speaker: clive
segments.text: Something down there. Something that shouldn't be.
segments.direction: Clive's warning, his ancient memories recognizing danger

segments.speaker: PAGE_TURN
segments.text: [DING - page turn cue]
segments.direction: Signals reader to turn page

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The crowd around the Pillar wasn't a mourning crowd. Three different guild banners—Cogwright, Steamfitter, Ironbound—and their representatives were already arguing about salvage rights. Guild Marshals stood at the perimeter, copper badges gleaming, maintaining order with the authority of corporate law. Nobody was looking up at the dying light. They were measuring distances, making calculations, laying claim.

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [GUILD ARGUMENT - overlapping voices, guild representatives in heated debate about salvage protocols]
segments.direction: Background voices showing corporate priorities over mourning

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: A young woman in Cogwright colors broke away from the argument, her face flushed with anger. I recognized the copper threading in her coat—junior guild member, probably in her First Forge year. She caught my eye and shook her head.

audio_cues.tone: observational, noting the idealist breaking away

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: They're talking about it like it's already scrap. Like it's just raw materials waiting to be harvested. Nobody remembers that this Pillar powered the district when the Senate didn't think we were worth the investment.
segments.direction: The young worker speaking to Pixel, her frustration and idealism showing

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [MOCHI VIBRATION - color shift from blue to violet, ancient recognition pulsing]
segments.direction: Ephergent device reacting to the situation with mysterious knowledge

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Mochi vibrated in my pouch, its glow shifting from blue to a deep, unsettling violet. The color of mystery. The color of ancient recognition.

audio_cues.tone: concerned, noticing Mochi's response to something larger

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Hey. What do you know that we don't?
segments.direction: Pixel questioning Mochi, seeking understanding

audio_cues.tone: gentle, curious, trying to connect with the device

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Mochi pulsed once, then went still. Not an answer. A refusal.

audio_cues.tone: realizing Mochi won't help, building dread

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: That was new. That scared me.

segments.speaker: MUSIC
segments.text: [MUSIC: Act Two - descending tension, moving underground, copper decay, Signal draining]
segments.direction: Transition to tunnel and mystery

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: We found the access tunnel beneath the Pillar's foundation—old maintenance passages that predated the current guild system. The copper plating on the walls showed guild marks from centuries past, some from guilds that no longer existed.

audio_cues.tone: exploratory, noticing the age and history

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [HOLOGRAM GLOW - Arc's wristband projecting soft blue light into copper-tinged darkness]
segments.direction: Arc providing illumination as they descend

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Arc guided us through, his wristband projecting a soft blue glow into the copper-tinged darkness.

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [TUNNEL AMBIENCE - water dripping with musical echoes, metal creaking, ancient pipes groaning, Signal energy crackling wrong]
segments.direction: Underground environment creating unease

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The tunnel walls were lined with pipes—ancient copper conduits green with patina, some still carrying faint traces of heat or pressure. Water dripped somewhere in the distance, each drop echoing with a musical quality that made me think of Nocturnis. The air smelled of wet metal and old grease, underlaid with something sharper. The smell of Signal energy going wrong.

audio_cues.tone: observational, noticing the wrongness, references other regions

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: Miss Paradox, I must confess something.
segments.direction: Arc breaking protocol, admission of something significant

audio_cues.tone: formal but strained, something important to share

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: I stopped. Arc never confessed things.

audio_cues.tone: surprised, realizing something's changed with Arc

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: These readings. They contain communication fragments that predate my own activation. By centuries, possibly millennia. I shouldn't be able to interpret them. And yet... I can.
segments.direction: Arc grappling with an impossible realization

audio_cues.tone: formal but uncertain, facing incomprehensible data

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: What do they say?
segments.direction: Pixel pressing for the revelation

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: A pause. When Arc paused, something was very wrong.

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: They say goodbye.
segments.direction: Arc's revelation, simple and devastating

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [DEVICE HUM - the siphoning device thrumming with ancient power, Signal flowing wrong direction]
segments.direction: The sabotage device revealed, pulling Signal energy away

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The tunnel opened into a chamber at the Pillar's base. The black stone rose above us, thrumming with weakening energy, copper conduits running down its surface like veins. And there, attached to its surface like a mechanical parasite, was a device I'd never seen before.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Copper coils wrapped around crystal cores. Brass housings covered in geometric patterns that matched Pillar markings. Tubes ran from the device into the stone itself, and through them, I could see light flowing away—the Pillar's Signal, being siphoned like blood from a wound.

audio_cues.tone: detailed observation of the sabotage mechanism, horror growing

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Someone's killing it.
segments.direction: Pixel's realization spoken aloud

segments.speaker: clive
segments.text: Not just killing. Erasing. I've seen this pattern before. Long time ago. Someone wants this Pillar to not just die—they want it forgotten.
segments.direction: Clive's ancient memory recognizing the technique, his voice gravelly and certain

audio_cues.tone: gravelly noir, recognizing old methods, grim

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: Miss Paradox, the device is... I recognize the construction. Not the device itself, but the principles. They're similar to my own underlying architecture. This shouldn't be possible. I was built forty-three years ago. This device is ancient.
segments.direction: Arc's shock and bewilderment, facing a connection he shouldn't have to ancient technology

audio_cues.tone: formal but disturbed, analytical trying to process the impossible
audio_cues.pacing: measured but with pauses of distress

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [TUNNEL ECHO - footsteps approaching, guild voices echoing down the passage, argument intensifying]
segments.direction: Guild masters arriving at the chamber

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: I was about to respond when voices echoed down the tunnel behind us. Guild masters, arguing as they approached.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Cogwright precedent clearly states that extraction rights belong to the guild whose district—
segments.direction: Torque's argument, part of the guild dispute

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Your precedent is two hundred years old, Torque. The Compact of Eighteen-Twelve supersedes—
segments.direction: Grindstone's counterargument

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The Compact applies to natural decay, not catastrophic failure—
segments.direction: Torque's response, escalating

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Three figures emerged into the chamber: Guildmaster Torque of the Cogwrights, her copper-threaded coat gleaming even in the dim light. Master Grindstone of the Steamfitters, his pressure-gauge monocle hissing softly. And Ironbound's representative, a younger woman named Ratchet whose hands were already stained with the black oil that marked her guild's work.

audio_cues.tone: introducing the guild masters, noting their characteristics

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: They stopped when they saw the device.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: By the Hammered One. What is that?
segments.direction: Torque's oath and question, reverence and shock

audio_cues.tone: shocked, reverent oath, professional facade cracking

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Evidence of sabotage. This Pillar didn't fail naturally. Someone murdered it.
segments.direction: Pixel revealing the truth to the guild masters

audio_cues.tone: professional, accusatory, laying out the crime

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Grindstone's monocle clicked through different lenses as he examined the device from a distance. Those coils... that's pre-Guild work. Maybe pre-Cuprum entirely. Who would have access to technology like that?
segments.direction: Grindstone analyzing, his voice coming through his examination

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Who would want to? The Pillars power everything. The Senate contracts depend on them. Destroying one is—it's incomprehensible. It's heresy against the Forge itself.
segments.direction: Ratchet's outburst, her idealism and shock

segments.speaker: clive
segments.text: Not to everyone. Some people think the Pillars need to die. Think it's mercy.
segments.direction: Clive's ominous statement, revealing the intentionality

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [PILLAR PULSE WEAKENING - Signal energy visibly draining, light fading, rhythmic death-knell]
segments.direction: The Pillar's final moments becoming audible

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Above us, the Pillar's pulse weakened visibly. We all felt it—that particular sensation of Signal energy draining away, like warmth leaving a room. Like the heat leaving a forge when the fire dies.

audio_cues.tone: visceral, observing the physical manifestation of death

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: Miss Paradox, I... this is difficult to articulate. I'm experiencing what I can only describe as distress. The Pillar's degradation is causing processing irregularities that shouldn't occur. I'm designed to observe and analyze, not to... not to feel loss.
segments.direction: Arc's emotional breakthrough, struggling with new feelings

audio_cues.tone: formal but fracturing, vulnerable, confused, experiencing emotion for first time

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: And yet I find myself grieving. For a structure of stone and Signal I've never directly interfaced with. This makes no logical sense.
segments.direction: Arc's acceptance of illogical grief

audio_cues.tone: quiet, resigned to emotion, formal voice breaking

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: I wanted to comfort him, but the guild masters were already arguing again—and this time, their personalities were showing through the stress.

audio_cues.tone: noticing the conflict resuming, unable to help Arc

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Sabotage or not, salvage protocols still apply. We have contracts. Obligations.
segments.direction: Torque's position, corporate pragmatism

audio_cues.tone: clipped, professional, no room for sentiment

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Obligations? You mean Senate quotas. You mean keeping the corporate republic happy so they don't revoke our charters.
segments.direction: Ratchet's sharp response, idealism clashing with reality

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Careful, Ratchet. The Senate hears everything eventually. Contracts keep our people employed. Philosophy doesn't.
segments.direction: Grindstone's weary wisdom, the voice of experience

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Listen to yourselves! A Pillar is dying. An actual Pillar. My grandmother swore her guild oath in its light. My father learned his craft by its glow. And all you can talk about is scrap value and Senate contracts?
segments.direction: Ratchet's passionate outburst

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: She turned away from them, facing the dying spire. In the dim light, I could see tears on her face.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The Hammered One watches our hands, not our prayers. That's what the Forge Path teaches. But what does it mean when our hands only reach for profit? When we melt down the very bones of what the First Smith built?
segments.direction: Ratchet's philosophical lament

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [PILLAR FINAL PULSE - light flickering out, Signal energy reaching critical depletion, moment of death]
segments.direction: The Pillar's last moments, visual and energetic death

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The Pillar pulsed again, weaker. The light that had illuminated generations was fading to ember-dim.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Mochi had gone completely still in my carrier, its glow so faint I could barely see it. Not pink. Not blue. A pale, washed-out grey I'd never seen before. When I touched it, the surface felt cool. Wrong.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Mochi?
segments.direction: Pixel reaching out to the device, anxious

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [MOCHI GRIEF PULSE - subsonic harmonic, deep tremors, pure sorrow, different from any sound before]
segments.direction: Ephergent device grieving in its own language

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: It didn't respond. Just vibrated with tremors so deep and slow I felt them more than heard them. A low, subsonic pulse of pure sorrow.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Can we stop it?
segments.direction: Pixel asking the question she already knows the answer to

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: Removing the device forcibly would accelerate the collapse. The Pillar has perhaps three hours remaining regardless. The damage is done. The only question now is whether we document how it was done, or let whoever did this remain anonymous.
segments.direction: Arc moving back into analytical mode, though the grief remains underneath

audio_cues.tone: analytical but mournful, formal facade returning

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Torque and Grindstone exchanged looks. Whatever their argument about salvage rights, they were guild leaders. They understood what was at stake. This wasn't just about their district anymore.

audio_cues.tone: noticing the guild leaders' shift in perspective

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Document everything. The Council will want to know. All the Councils will want to know. If someone's targeting Pillars, every guild across Cuprum needs to prepare.
segments.direction: Torque's new priority, shifting from salvage to security

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Then we make sure they remember. The device, the construction, the method. Whoever did this—we make sure they can't hide.
segments.direction: Pixel committing to the documentation

segments.speaker: PAGE_TURN
segments.text: [DING - page turn cue]
segments.direction: Signals reader to turn page

segments.speaker: MUSIC
segments.text: [MUSIC: Act Three - requiem for the Pillar, loss and determination, documentary mood]
segments.direction: Transition to documentation and resolution

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: We worked fast. Arc analyzed the device's construction, cataloging every component. I recorded everything - the chamber, the Pillar's fading light, the way the shadows shifted as its pulse grew weaker. Clive pointed me toward symbols on the device's housing, ancient markings that matched fragments from his incomplete memories.

audio_cues.tone: focused, professional documentation, team working together

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Seen these before. The ones who came before... they had enemies. Maybe they still do.
segments.direction: Clive's enigmatic observation

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [PILLAR DEATH - final Signal drain, light extinguishing, Pillar going dark, the moment of death]
segments.direction: The Verdance Pillar dies, dramatic moment

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The Pillar died an hour before dawn.

audio_cues.tone: matter-of-fact statement of fact, but heavy

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: I'd seen Pillars go dark before, but always from a distance. Never like this—standing at its base, feeling the last pulse of Signal wash over me like a final breath. The light didn't fade. It simply stopped, between one heartbeat and the next.

audio_cues.tone: intimate with death, visceral, experiencing the moment

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Mochi went still.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Not its glow—that remained, but dimmed to a pale, faded blue I'd never seen before. The warm surface grew cool in my hands. For one terrible moment, I thought I'd lost it. Then it vibrated once, trembling, and let out a long descending tone—the kind of sound that doesn't need translation.

audio_cues.tone: describing Mochi's grief, intimate with the device

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Loss. That's what it felt like. Pure, wordless loss.

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Mochi... Did you sense something? A connection to the Pillar?
segments.direction: Pixel asking gently, trying to understand

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Mochi pulsed once, weakly. I couldn't read the color—it was too dim, too uncertain. But the way it responded to my touch, the way it resonated with grief, told me everything I needed to know about what Ephergents feel when a Pillar dies.

audio_cues.tone: understanding Ephergent nature, recognition of profound connection

segments.speaker: arc
segments.text: Miss Paradox. I don't understand why this loss affects me as it does. My records indicate no personal connection to this Pillar. And yet...
segments.direction: Arc struggling with grief, his formal voice barely holding

audio_cues.tone: confused, vulnerable, formal facade cracking completely

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: He trailed off. I didn't push. Sometimes even an AI needed time to process the impossible.

segments.speaker: clive
segments.text: They're going to try again. Whoever did this. Whatever they call themselves. This wasn't random. This was practice.
segments.direction: Clive's ominous prophecy

segments.speaker: SFX
segments.text: [CUPRUM SHIFT-BELL - early morning shift change, returning to normal industrial rhythm, but with absence]
segments.direction: The world continuing despite the Pillar's death

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Above us, the Patina Quarter began to wake to a world with one less light. I could hear the shift-bell ringing—the start of the morning shift—and knew that workers would emerge to find their Pillar dark. The guild Chaplains would need to recite the words for a dead forge. The district would have to petition the Senate for power redistribution, beg for resources that the corporate republic would dole out according to guild influence and political favor.

audio_cues.tone: observing consequences, world-weary understanding of bureaucracy

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The guilds would strip the Pillar for parts within days, their salvage crews working under contract law and council mandate. But we'd recorded everything. The device, the sabotage, the patterns that matched Clive's fragmented memories and Arc's impossible recognition.

audio_cues.tone: documenting the action, noting what they've accomplished

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: Someone was killing Pillars on purpose. And somewhere in our team, we carried pieces of the answer.

audio_cues.tone: realizing the larger threat, shifting from one Pillar to a pattern

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: We just didn't know which pieces yet.

segments.speaker: PAGE_TURN
segments.text: [DING - page turn cue]
segments.direction: Signals reader to turn page

segments.speaker: MUSIC
segments.text: [MUSIC: Chronicle theme - intimate, reflective, Pixel's personal voice, haunting melody]
segments.direction: Transition to Pixel's personal journal

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: chronicle entry: The Verdance Pillar of Cuprum's Patina Quarter died at 5:47 AM local time, seventeen hours after our arrival. Cause of death: deliberate Signal extraction via device of unknown origin. The device's construction predates all known Aethon technology, yet shares architectural principles with modern AI systems - specifically, with Arc's own underlying design.
segments.direction: Pixel's documented entry, clinical but with underlying emotion

audio_cues.tone: documentary, clinical facts mixed with personal grief, tired

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: When the Pillar died, Mochi mourned. Not like an observer. Like someone who had lost family.
segments.direction: Personal observation of Ephergent grief

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: I'm supposed to document these things objectively. Record the facts, preserve the data, move on to the next job. But watching Arc struggle to understand his own grief, feeling Mochi's sorrow resonate through my hands, I realized something: we're not just collecting information anymore. We're uncovering truth that someone desperately wants buried.
segments.direction: Pixel's realization about their mission expanding

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: The guilds call whoever did this The Quiet, if the rumors are true. They kill Pillars and erase knowledge, all in the name of protecting Aethon from... what? Remembering?
segments.direction: Pixel introducing the threat

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: They'll find that some things refuse to stay silent.
segments.direction: Final line of defiance and determination

segments.speaker: pixel
segments.text: -P.P.
segments.direction: Pixel's signature
